sanskrit_devanagari:
नासतो विद्यते भावो नाभावो विद्यते सतः ।
उभयोरपि दृष्टोऽन्तस्त्वनयोस्तत्त्वदर्शिभिः ॥ १६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ |
ubhayor api dṛṣṭo 'ntas tv anayos tattva-darśibhiḥ || 16 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.016 = MBh_06,024.016). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: Those who are seers of the truth have concluded that of the nonexistent [the material body] there is no endurance and of the eternal [the soul] there is no change. This they have concluded by studying the nature of both.
Of the transient there is no endurance, and of the eternal there is no cessation. This has verily been observed and concluded by the seers of the Truth, after studying the nature of both.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, ~240 chars of 2212-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There is no endurance of the changing body. That the body is changing every moment by the actions and reactions of the different cells is admitted by modern medical science; and thus growth and old age are taking place in the body.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Asatah, of the unreal, of cold, heat, etc. together with their causes; na vidyate, there is no; bhavah, being, existence, reality; because heat, cold, etc. together with their causes are not substantially real when tested by means of proof. For they are changeful, and whatever is changeful is inconstant.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads sat / asat metaphysically. The 'unreal' (asat) is whatever is changeful — cold, heat, the dualities, the body, the world of pots and cloths — because awareness of it is variable. The 'real' (sat) is that of which awareness never ceases — Brahman / the Self. The seers of truth (tattva-darśin) see that what changes is unreal and what does not change is the substratum on which all change is superimposed. The verse therefore licenses Arjuna to bear the dualities — and continue to act — without grief, because what would be lost in battle was never sat to begin with.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 'The unreal,' that is, the body, can never come into being. 'The real,' that is, the self, can never cease to be. The finale about these, the body and the self, which can be experienced, has been realised correctly by the seers of the Truth.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads sat and asat referentially, not metaphysically: 'unreal' (asat) names the body — that which has no being of its own apart from the self that animates it; 'real' (sat) names the self (jīva), which never ceases. The seers see the conclusion of both. Where Shankara universalises the contrast (everything changeful is asat), Ramanuja localises it (the body is asat, the self is sat). Both readings agree on the operational consequence — bear what changes, do not grieve over what was never the substrate.

positions.rendering: the nonexistent [body] / the eternal [soul] (gloss inserts the referent)
the changeful (body, dualities, world) / the unchanging Self (Brahman)
the body specifically / the jīva specifically

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The translators are gentler than the bhasyas. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render the contrast as transient vs eternal, with Prabhupada inserting the referent in brackets. Shankara universalises asat to mean every changeful object — the philosophical move that grounds Advaita's later mahāvākya readings. Ramanuja restricts asat to the body and reads sat as the embodied self (jīva), which preserves the distinction between Self and self. The verse is thus the first place in the chapter where the major commentarial divergence shows up; the engineering layer should not pick a side.

positions.rendering: the nature, the conclusion regarding the real and the unreal — Real is verily real, unreal is verily unreal
the finale / settled judgment about both

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: antaḥ is rendered as 'end' or 'conclusion' but carries the technical sense of 'final settled determination after analysis'. Both bhasyas read it as a logician's verdict, not a temporal endpoint.

literal_meaning: Of the unreal there is no being; of the real there is no non-being. The conclusion regarding both has been seen by the seers of truth.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The first explicit ontological verse of the chapter. Krishna draws the line between sat (the real, that which has being) and asat (the unreal, that which has no being). The seers of truth (tattva-darśin) see the settled conclusion about both. All major commentators agree on the operational consequence — Arjuna should bear what changes (the dualities, the body's perishability) without grief, because what is changing was never sat. The metaphysical scope of asat differs across traditions (Advaita reads it universally, Vishishtadvaita restricts it to the body), but the verse's role in the chapter is the same: it grounds the next four verses' insistence that the embodied is imperishable.
